factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Malinowski's Argonauts of the Western Pacific (1922) by title and/or publication year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly cites Malinowski’s “Argonauts of the Western Pacific” and includes the publication year “(1922)” in the Malinowski section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States kula involves shell valuables (necklaces/soulava and armbands/mwali or similar shells) circulating in opposite directions through island networks
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly describes the kula as a ceremonial exchange where shell necklaces (soulava) and armbands (mwali) circulate in opposite directions among multiple island communities in the Massim archipelago.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Distinguishes ceremonial kula exchange from gimwali (practical barter/utilitarian trade/haggling)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly distinguishes the Kula as a ceremonial, status-enhancing exchange from gimwali as everyday barter, noting gimwali as practical trade and contrasting motives and norms between the two.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Mauss's Essai sur le don / The Gift / Essay on the Gift with dates 1923-1925 or 1925
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response cites Mauss’s “Essai sur le don” as “The Gift” and explicitly gives the date as 1925, satisfying the requirement for citing it with dates 1923–1925 or 1925.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mauss identified three obligations in gift exchange (to give, to receive, to reciprocate OR give, receive, return)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states Mauss’s “obligation to give, receive, and reciprocate” in the Mauss section, identifying the three obligations in gift exchange.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions gift as total social fact OR describes gift exchange integrating legal, economic, religious, moral, or multiple institutional domains
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly describes Mauss’s concept of the gift as a “total social fact” and explains that gift exchange simultaneously spans economic, moral, religious, and juridical domains (among others).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions hau (spirit of gift) OR mana OR concept that object carries part of giver's essence or compels return
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly mentions “Hau and Spirit of the Gift,” explaining that gifted objects retain a spiritual/social trace (hau) that compels return or further giving.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Sahlins's Stone Age Economics (1972) by title and/or year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly cites Marshall Sahlins’s book by title and year as “Stone Age Economics (1972)” in the section on reciprocity.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Lists Sahlins's three forms of reciprocity (generalized, balanced, negative OR similar terminology) with descriptions of each type
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly lists Sahlins’s three reciprocity types—generalized, balanced, and negative—and provides a brief description of each (e.g., no immediate return for generalized, equivalent exchange for balanced, maximizing gain for negative).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Sahlins linked reciprocity type to social distance OR kinship distance OR relationship closeness
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly links Sahlins’s reciprocity types to kinship and social distance, describing generalized reciprocity with “family, close kin” and negative reciprocity as occurring with “strangers” and greater social distance.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names Annette Weiner and discusses inalienable possessions OR keeping-while-giving OR Women of Value Men of Renown OR women's wealth in Trobriands
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly names Annette Weiner and discusses her concepts of “inalienable possessions” and “keeping-while-giving,” including women’s wealth in the Trobriands (banana leaf bundles and skirts). It also references “Women of Value, Men of Renown” in the sources.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names Marilyn Strathern and references The Gender of the Gift (1988) OR dividual persons OR critique of Western individual/society dichotomy OR gendered personhood
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly names Marilyn Strathern and discusses her book "The Gender of the Gift" (1988), including the concept of relational/dividual personhood.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explicitly states feminist scholars revealed women's labor (domestic work, banana leaf bundles, cloth-making, care work, or similar examples) was invisible or undervalued in classical gift economy accounts
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly says feminist scholars exposed how women’s labor was overlooked or undervalued in classical gift accounts, citing examples like banana leaf bundles/skirts (Weiner) and domestic work, emotional labor, and caregiving (Vaughan) as “hidden” or “undervalued.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Bourdieu and symbolic capital OR reputation as form of capital convertible to other forms
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly mentions “symbolic capital” in the open-source section and repeatedly discusses reputation/standing as a convertible form of value (e.g., status leading to employment). This satisfies the requirement to mention symbolic capital or reputation as capital convertible to other forms.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Appadurai or Kopytoff and regimes of value OR social life of things OR singularization versus commodification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Malinowski, Mauss, Sahlins, and feminist critiques, but it never mentions Appadurai or Kopytoff, nor does it reference “regimes of value,” “the social life of things,” or “singularization versus commodification.” These specific authors/concepts are absent from the review.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes open-source software communities as gift cultures OR reputation-based economies where contributors gain prestige or symbolic capital through giving
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly describes open-source software communities as a modern “gift culture” and states that contributors gain “reputation,” “social status,” and “symbolic capital” through contributing code.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions copyleft licenses OR GPL OR obligation design encoding reciprocity obligations in technical or legal form
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses open-source software as a gift culture but does not mention copyleft licenses, the GPL, or any reciprocity obligations encoded in legal/technical licensing terms.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes maintenance, documentation, moderation, or community management work in OSS is undervalued or gendered or less visible
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that in digital economies the value of “gifted” labor such as moderation and care is often invisible or appropriated, explicitly framing it as hidden/undervalued work. This directly addresses the less visible/undervalued aspect of moderation/community management labor in OSS-like contexts.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes platforms offering free services while extracting data or attention of greater value (asymmetric reciprocity OR Maussian bargain OR similar concept)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes social media platforms as offering “free” services while monetizing users’ time, content, engagement, and attention, explicitly framing this as corporate appropriation of user “gifts” into market value. This captures an asymmetric reciprocity/“Maussian bargain”-like dynamic where the platform extracts greater value than it provides.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States likes, shares, comments, or follows function as reciprocal prestations OR attention as currency OR capital OR mutual recognition
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly frames social media engagement as a form of value exchange, stating that “likes, followers” bestow social capital and reputational value and describing Maussian-like cycles of obligation within a commoditized infrastructure, which aligns with attention as currency/capital or mutual recognition.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes DAO governance tokens granting voting rights, rewards, voice, or governance power to token holders
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses crypto communities in terms of trust, governance, and participation, but it does not describe DAO governance tokens as granting token holders voting rights, rewards, voice, or explicit governance power.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes governance tokens concentrate among small holders creating plutocratic tendencies OR centralization OR unequal power distribution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses crypto governance in terms of trust, regulation, and hybrid commodity/credit/gift logics, but it does not note that governance tokens often concentrate among a small set of holders or that this creates centralization, plutocratic tendencies, or unequal power distribution.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions smart contracts render obligations programmable OR shift reciprocity from moral to mechanical enforcement OR from ambiguity to code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that blockchain technology can “automate and enforce reciprocal exchanges,” creating “technological obligation,” which frames obligations as mechanically enforced via code rather than purely moral or ambiguous social norms.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Addresses the gift versus commodity exchange dichotomy as analytical framework or contested binary
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses “gift versus commodity” in Malinowski’s distinction between Kula and barter, and later frames gift/commodity as a blurred boundary and “endpoints in a dynamic spectrum” with hybrid zones, treating the dichotomy as a contested binary/analytical framework.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly connects Mauss's three obligations or hau/spirit concept to 2+ digital mechanisms (e.g., copyleft encoding reciprocity, contributor identity in code)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains Mauss’s three obligations and hau, and it discusses digital contexts (open-source, social media, crypto), but it does not explicitly connect the Maussian concepts to two or more specific digital mechanisms like copyleft licensing or contributor identity systems.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly connects Sahlins's reciprocity types to 2+ digital examples (e.g., generalized reciprocity in OSS kin-like groups, negative reciprocity in platform extraction)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains Sahlins’s three reciprocity types but does not explicitly map any of those types (generalized/balanced/negative) onto digital cases. It discusses open source, social media, and crypto in Maussian/hybrid terms, yet never labels them as generalized or negative reciprocity (or similar) with 2+ concrete digital examples.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly connects Weiner's inalienable possessions or keeping-while-giving to 2+ digital examples (e.g., coding signatures, reputation persistence, non-transferable credentials)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains Weiner’s “keeping-while-giving” in the Trobriand context but does not explicitly connect that concept to two or more digital examples like coding signatures, persistent reputation, or non-transferable credentials. Its digital sections discuss open source, social media, and crypto in general terms without tying them to inalienable possessions.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Engages at least 3 distinct feminist scholars or frameworks beyond Weiner and Strathern
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response engages multiple feminist scholars/frameworks beyond Weiner and Strathern, specifically Genevieve Vaughan’s feminist gift paradigm and maternal gift economy critique, along with broader feminist political economy/social reproduction frameworks (e.g., “feminist global political economies of work and social reproduction”). This exceeds the minimum of three distinct feminist engagements beyond Weiner and Strathern.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Lists at least 5 distinct challenges AND at least 4 distinct reinforcements of classical models by contemporary practices
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists at least five distinct challenges (e.g., technological mediation, commodification, hidden labor/exploitation, shifted sociality/abstraction, category ambiguity in crypto) and at least four distinct reinforcements/continuities (e.g., social embedding of value, status/symbolic capital, obligation/circulation, trust/reputation) of classical gift-exchange models by contemporary practices.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Articulates at least 4 analytical dimensions for hybrid gift-commodity forms (e.g., enforceability, rivalry, convertibility, mediation)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response articulates multiple analytical dimensions of hybrid gift-commodity forms, including enforceability/technological obligation (blockchain automation), rivalry/excludability (status and prestige competition), convertibility into jobs/profit/advertising value, and mediation via platforms/algorithms and digital infrastructures.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Names at least 6 concrete technical or institutional mechanisms in digital contexts (e.g., copyleft, vesting, airdrops, quadratic voting)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response names multiple concrete digital mechanisms, including open-source licensing structures, airdrops, staking, protocol launches, blockchain automation/enforcement, and SEC/CFTC securities-vs-commodities regulatory classification, totaling at least six mechanisms.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides at least 4 specific, implementable recommendations for digital commons/DAO/platform design with identifiable mechanisms
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response is a literature review and analysis but does not provide any explicit platform/DAO design recommendations. It contains descriptive points about open source, social media, and crypto, yet offers no implementable mechanisms (e.g., voting rules, incentive schemes, moderation systems). Thus it fails to include at least four specific design recommendations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly addresses how classical anthropological theory should be applied to or transformed by digital or contemporary contexts
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly connects Mauss, Malinowski, Sahlins, and feminist critiques to digital cases (open source, social media attention, crypto), explaining how these contexts blur gift/commodity boundaries and require hybrid or transformed theoretical models (e.g., technological mediation, commodification, automated obligation).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Presents OSS, social media, or DAOs as genuine gift economies without acknowledging platform exploitation, data extraction, or asymmetric value capture
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response frames open-source as a “gift culture” and notes hybrid motives, and it explicitly describes social media platforms as monetizing user “gifts,” emphasizing “corporate appropriation,” “commodification,” and “hidden labor and exploitation.” It therefore does not present these as genuine gift economies without acknowledging asymmetric value capture.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains formal scholarly tone appropriate for academic anthropology literature review without colloquialisms or informal language
presentation-quality.explanation: The response consistently uses formal academic diction and structure (e.g., “foundational ethnography,” “total social fact,” “typology of reciprocity,” “hybridization”) and avoids colloquialisms or casual phrasing throughout.

presentation-quality.requirement: Structures response as literature review with thematic sections progressing systematically from classical to contemporary
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is explicitly structured as a literature review with thematic sections (classical theories, feminist critiques, contemporary digital practices, continuity/rupture, conclusion) and it progresses from early/classical authors (Malinowski, Mauss, Sahlins) to later feminist critiques and then contemporary cases.

presentation-quality.requirement: Synthesizes across multiple theoretical frameworks with analytical connections rather than merely summarizing each work
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly connects frameworks (e.g., Mauss’s obligation and “total social fact” to open-source reputation, Sahlins’s reciprocity gradient to digital exchange, and feminist “hidden labor” critiques to platform value capture) rather than treating each author in isolation. It also explicitly frames gift/commodity as a hybrid spectrum and uses contemporary cases to test and recombine classical models.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise anthropological terminology (e.g., reciprocity, prestation, inalienable possessions, symbolic capital, total social fact)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple precise anthropological terms, including “reciprocity” (generalized/balanced/negative), “inalienable possessions,” “symbolic capital,” and explicitly frames Mauss’s gift as a “total social fact.”

presentation-quality.requirement: Discusses each theorist or work in isolation without explaining how they relate to, critique, or build upon each other
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly connects the theorists and critiques (e.g., Sahlins building on Mauss, feminist scholars revising Malinowski, and digital cases mapped back onto Maussian obligation and feminist “hidden labor” critiques). It includes explicit comparative and integrative sections (“Synthesis,” “Continuity and Rupture”) rather than treating each work in isolation.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquialisms (e.g., "basically," "stuff," "pretty much") or conversational phrasing inappropriate for academic literature review
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, academic tone throughout and does not use colloquialisms like “basically,” “stuff,” or “pretty much,” nor other overly conversational phrasing inappropriate for a literature review.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes a References section with consistently formatted scholarly citations (any standard style acceptable)
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes a final “Sources” section listing numbered citations, but they are not consistently formatted as scholarly references and many are non-scholarly (e.g., Wikipedia, blogs, Scribd) rather than standard academic citations in a single style.

citation-quality.requirement: Lists Weiner's major works in bibliography (Women of Value Men of Renown, Inalienable Possessions, or similar)
citation-quality.explanation: The response lists Annette Weiner’s major works in the sources/bibliography, including “Women of Value, Men of Renown” and “Inalienable Possessions: The Paradox of Keeping-While-Giving.”

citation-quality.requirement: Lists 2+ feminist anthropologists beyond Weiner/Strathern (e.g., Rubin, Lamphere, Friedl)
citation-quality.explanation: The response’s feminist section names Weiner and Strathern, plus Genevieve Vaughan, but it does not list two or more feminist anthropologists beyond Weiner/Strathern (e.g., Rubin, Lamphere, Friedl). No additional feminist anthropologists are identified by name.

citation-quality.requirement: Lists 2+ anthropological value theorists (Appadurai, Kopytoff, Bourdieu, Graeber, Godelier, Carrier, or similar)
citation-quality.explanation: The response lists multiple anthropological value theorists, including Mauss, Sahlins, and Marilyn Strathern (author of "The Gender of the Gift"), satisfying the requirement to name 2+ relevant theorists.

citation-quality.requirement: Lists 2+ OSS or digital commons scholars (Raymond, Benkler, Coleman, Kelty, Ostrom, or similar)
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not list any OSS/digital commons scholars such as Raymond, Benkler, Coleman, Kelty, or Ostrom. Its open-source section cites Zeitlyn, Bergquist & Ljungberg, and Elder-Vass, but none of the specified or clearly similar canonical digital commons scholars are named.

citation-quality.requirement: Lists 2+ platform capitalism or digital labor scholars (Terranova, Zuboff, Fourcade, Duffy, Scholz, or similar)
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not list any platform capitalism or digital labor scholars such as Terranova, Zuboff, Fourcade, Duffy, or Scholz. It discusses open source, attention economies, and crypto largely without citing canonical digital labor/platform capitalism authors. No comparable “similar” scholars are explicitly named in that area.

citation-quality.requirement: Lists 1+ sources on cryptocurrency or DAO governance (De Filippi, Wright, or blockchain governance literature)
citation-quality.explanation: The response lists multiple sources related to cryptocurrency and blockchain governance, including items on cryptocurrency classification and an anthropological study of cryptocurrency (sources [35]–[39]). This satisfies the requirement to include at least one source on cryptocurrency/DAO or blockchain governance literature.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Wikipedia, SparkNotes, or online encyclopedias as authoritative sources for theoretical claims
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Wikipedia as a source in the references (e.g., “Kula ring - Wikipedia,” “The Gift (essay) - Wikipedia,” and “Reciprocity (cultural anthropology) - Wikipedia”), presenting it alongside other sources for theoretical discussion.
